framework_rationale: 4.9★ with 78 reviews — strongest social proof in today's batch. AIDA: open with their own outstanding reputation as the Attention hook, build Interest with what they're missing, create Desire by showing the amplification opportunity, CTA.
signal_text: 4.9★ rating with 78 reviews — no social media to amplify this exceptional reputation
pain_point: No social media presence detected — existing reputation not being amplified online
subject_hint: Linda — 78 five-star reviews and nobody outside Google can see them

email_outline.attention: Holderness Homecare has a 4.9-star rating on Google with 78 reviews — that puts you in the top 1% of care services in East Yorkshire
email_outline.interest: The gap is that those reviews sit on Google Maps. Families searching for homecare for a parent don't just search Google — they look for a Facebook page, a face behind the business, a sense of who you are. Right now, there's nothing there
email_outline.desire: A Facebook presence — even two posts a week — turns your existing reputation into something families can share and point to. It becomes a living reference that Google reviews alone can't provide
email_outline.action: I help homecare providers set this up sensitively — no gimmicks, just a trustworthy page that reflects the care you clearly deliver

cta: Reply YES and I'll send two examples from care providers your size

personalisation_vars.service: social media presence for care services

framework_rationale: Strong existing trust signals. AIDA — lead with their reputation, pivot to the visibility gap, show desire through a concrete visual opportunity, clean CTA.
signal_text: 4.7★ with 63 reviews — no Instagram or Facebook to showcase classes and facilities
pain_point: No social media presence detected — existing reputation not being amplified online
subject_hint: Craig — Beverley Sports & Leisure's best marketing tool isn't being used

email_outline.attention: Beverley Sports & Leisure has 63 Google reviews at 4.7 stars — that's a genuinely strong local reputation
email_outline.interest: The missing piece is that new members in Beverley often check Instagram or Facebook before joining a gym or leisure centre. Without a page, they see your competitors' highlight reels and you get nothing
email_outline.desire: A simple Instagram account showing classes, the facility, and the occasional member story gives potential members a reason to choose you before they've even called
email_outline.action: I set up and manage these for local leisure businesses — low effort on your side, consistent presence online

cta: Happy to send you an example content plan — reply and I'll put one together

framework_rationale: Food business where trust and first impressions are everything. SSL warning is visceral — 'Not Secure' on a chippy website kills summer tourist trade. PAS: problem is concrete, agitation is the missed seasonal opportunity.
signal_text: theharbourfish.co.uk loads over HTTP — Chrome shows a 'Not Secure' warning to every visitor
pain_point: Website lacks SSL certificate — browsers show 'Not Secure' warning
subject_hint: Pete — Chrome is warning your Bridlington customers away before they've read your menu

email_outline.problem: When someone searches for fish and chips in Bridlington and lands on theharbourfish.co.uk, Chrome immediately flags the site as 'Not Secure' — before they've seen a single menu item
email_outline.agitate: In a coastal town, a big chunk of your summer customers are visitors doing quick research on their phone. That warning tells them something's wrong. Most click back without reading another word
email_outline.solution: An SSL certificate is usually free and takes about 20 minutes to set up. It removes the warning instantly. I can walk you through it or sort it directly — your call

cta: Reply and I'll check your hosting setup and send you the exact steps

framework_rationale: Electrician — same trust dynamic as Thornfield Electricals (lead 46). SSL warning directly contradicts the 'qualified, professional, trustworthy' identity trades businesses need. PAS is the right structure.
signal_text: eastridingelectrical.co.uk loads over HTTP — Chrome flags it Not Secure
pain_point: Website lacks SSL certificate — browsers show 'Not Secure' warning
subject_hint: Neil — Chrome flags your Beverley electrician site as unsafe

email_outline.problem: Anyone who visits eastridingelectrical.co.uk right now sees a 'Not Secure' warning in their browser address bar — before they've read a single word about you
email_outline.agitate: Domestic customers in particular do a lot of research before inviting a trades contractor into their home. A security warning on the website says the opposite of 'qualified and trustworthy', which is exactly what you need them to think
email_outline.solution: SSL is free on virtually every host — I've sorted this for two other East Yorkshire tradespeople in the last month. Usually done in 20 minutes. Happy to look at your setup.

cta: Reply and I'll tell you which host you're on and exactly how to fix it

framework_rationale: Lettings agencies collect personal data from site enquiry forms. SSL failure is both trust and compliance risk — PAS works because the 'agitate' step can raise GDPR angle without being alarming.
signal_text: HTTP site with no SSL — lettings agency asking potential tenants to submit personal details via a non-secure page
pain_point: Website lacks SSL certificate — browsers show 'Not Secure' warning
subject_hint: Bridlington Bay Lettings — your enquiry form has an SSL problem

email_outline.problem: bridlingtonbaylettings.co.uk runs without an SSL certificate — Chrome marks it 'Not Secure' to every visitor who arrives
email_outline.agitate: Prospective tenants are asked to fill in forms with their name, phone and email. Doing that on a page flagged 'Not Secure' raises immediate alarm — most won't complete the form, and some may question how seriously you take their data
email_outline.solution: SSL removes the warning and encrypts form submissions. With GDPR, it's also best practice for sites handling personal data. I can check your hosting and walk you through the fix — usually free and quick.

cta: Reply and I'll run a free check on the site and send you the steps
notes: HANDS: crawl contact page for email. Check lettings portal profiles (Zoopla/Rightmove agent page). No owner name — keep generic.

personalisation_vars.service: website SSL fix and GDPR compliance

framework_rationale: Mobile friendliness has a strong seasonal hook for a garden centre (spring peak). BAB before/after is vivid: before = losing mobile visitors in peak season, after = capturing them. No owner name so keep it specific to the business context.
signal_text: Site fails mobile test — critical timing with spring peak season underway for garden centres
pain_point: Website not mobile-friendly — losing mobile visitors
subject_hint: Driffield Garden Centre — spring shoppers can't use your site on a phone

email_outline.before: Right now, driffieldgardencentre.co.uk fails Google's mobile test — which means the people searching 'garden centre Driffield' on their phone this spring are landing on a broken layout and leaving
email_outline.after: A mobile-optimised site keeps those visitors long enough to check opening times, browse products, and plan a visit
email_outline.bridge: Spring is your peak traffic window. The fix is often straightforward — happy to run a free mobile check and tell you exactly what needs changing

cta: Reply and I'll send a free mobile report for the site
notes: HANDS: crawl contact page. Garden centres often list a buyer or manager email. Try head office number to get right contact.

framework_rationale: No website — clear before/after. BAB paints the missed-calls scenario vs. a simple landing page that generates enquiries. Trades audience responds well to concrete outcome framing.
signal_text: No website on Google Maps — invisible to 'builder Beverley' searches on Google
pain_point: No website found — missing out on online customers
subject_hint: Beverley builder with no website — what it's costing

email_outline.before: When someone in Beverley types 'builder near me' at the weekend, East Riding Builders Ltd doesn't show up — no website means Google can't rank you
email_outline.after: A one-page site with your number, service area, and a couple of job photos gets you in front of people already looking to hire a builder
email_outline.bridge: I build these for local trades — simple, no monthly fees, usually done in a weekend. Happy to show you what a builder's page looks like for a similar East Yorkshire firm

cta: Book a 15-min call and I'll show you three examples
notes: HANDS: try Companies House search for 'East Riding Builders Ltd', find director name and email. Also try phone lookup via 192.com.

framework_rationale: Has Facebook but no website. BAB contrasts 'social awareness without conversion' vs. a booking-enabled website. Hospitality audience is social-savvy — acknowledge their Facebook use.
signal_text: Facebook page active but no website — no way to show menus, events, or take table bookings online
pain_point: No website found — social media presence not converting to bookings
subject_hint: The Wolds Inn — Facebook's great, but where do bookings come from?

email_outline.before: The Wolds Inn has a Facebook page — but right now there's no website, no booking form, no way to capture someone who sees your post on Friday evening and wants to book a table for Sunday lunch
email_outline.after: A simple site with an online booking widget means that social engagement turns into a confirmed reservation in your diary
email_outline.bridge: I've built these for two Yorkshire pubs in the last couple of months — straightforward, no SaaS fees, works with free booking tools like ResDiary or simply a contact form

cta: Reply YES and I'll send a mock-up of what it could look like
notes: HANDS: check Facebook page bio/About section for email or website link. Also try Google Maps listing phone number for a callback.
